content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 10, 2020

John Abaci
Assistant City Attorney
Walter & Pistole
670 W. Napa St., Suite F
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-184

Dear Mr. Abaci:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sonoma City Mayor Logan Harvey regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Mayor Harvey from taking part in decisions relating to Verizon's proposed streetlight structure containing wireless telecommunications equipment stored inside the structure and located within 500 feet of the apartment he leases on a month-to-month basis?

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, Mayor Harvey's month-to-month tenancy in the apartment does qualify as a real property interest under the Act.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are an Assistant City Attorney authorized to request this advice on behalf of Sonoma City Mayor Harvey who lives in an apartment on West Napa Street in Sonoma which he rents. His lease is currently on a month-to-month basis. Rent is adjusted each year by the landlord.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-184
Page No. 2

Mayor Harvey's rental unit is located on the second floor, to the rear and above Duggan's
Funeral Home. An auto repair facility is located directly adjacent to the east of Duggan's Funeral
Home on West Napa Street. Directly south of the auto repair business is a U.S. Bank branch
building located at 552 Fifth Street West.

Verizon Wireless has made an application to the City to place a metal pole approximately 30
feet tall with a streetlight and wireless telecommunications equipment for the transmission of
wireless signals on the front of the sidewalk area along Fifth Street West between the auto repair
and bank buildings. The wireless equipment would consist of the following: a radio unit, canister
antenna, PG&E Smart Meter and related support equipment. The radio unit would be mounted on
the outside of the pole; however, the Planning Commission has imposed a condition of approval
that the radio unit be placed in an underground vault. The canister antenna and PG&E Smart Meter
would be placed within a cylindrical shroud atop the pole and thus not be visible. There would also
be an electrical disconnection panel on the pole. The streetlight would be directed downwards
toward the street.² The streetlight structure would be located within 500 feet of Mayor Harvey's
residence.

The City is approximately 2 square miles in area. Its population is estimated to be above
11,000. Under Chapter 5.32 of the Sonoma Municipal Code, a wireless telecommunications facility
needs a use permit to use a location within the City. Therefore, the proposed streetlight structure
will require a use permit to be allowed. The City's Planning Commission heard the application for
the use permit on January 23, 2020. The application was granted subject to the condition that
Verizon place the radio not on the outside of the pole but rather within an underground vault in an
area in close proximity to the streetlight structure. Verizon is appealing that condition, as well as
one other condition that would not directly impact the approved design. Verizon also amended its
application in advance of the hearing at the request of the Planning Department to relocate three
signs (school crossing and bus stop) from their present location on a nearby sign pole to the
streetlight pole which would result in shielding the electrical disconnection panel from the view of
oncoming traffic. The appeal will be heard by the City Council on March 2020.

Verizon Wireless submitted final application materials on December 20, 2019 proposing the
installation of three streetlight structures, containing wireless telecommunications equipment stored
inside the structures, to be placed within the sidewalk areas at three different locations in the City.
Application for Site No. 006 is the application that is nearest to Mayor Harvey's residence.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

___________________________
² You provided a simulation of the pole design and location submitted by Verizon.

File No. A-19-184
Page No. 3

Under Section 87103(b), an official has a financial interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. The Act defines a real property interest to include a leasehold interest. (Section 82033.) However, Regulation 18233 expressly provides that the statute's terms "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest" do not include the interest of a tenant in a month-to-month lease. For purposes of the Act's conflict of interest rules, therefore, Mayor Harvey's month-to-month apartment lease is not itself "an interest in real property."

Accordingly, Mayor Harvey does not have financial interest resulting in disqualification because his lease is only month-to-month.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Mayor Harvey from taking part in 
decisions relating to Verizon's proposed streetlight structure containing wireless 
telecommunications equipment stored inside the structure and located within 500 feet of the 
apartment he leases on a month-to-month basis?
sections.conclusion:
No. As explained below, Mayor Harvey's month-to-month tenancy in the apartment does 
qualify as a real property interest under the Act.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
2 You provided a simulation of the pole design and location submitted by Verizon.  
Under Section 87103(b), an official has a financial interest in any real property in which the 
official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. The Act defines a real property interest to 
include a leasehold interest. (Section 82033.) However, Regulation 18233 expressly provides that

Accordingly, Mayor Harvey does not have financial interest resulting in disqualification 
because his lease is only month-to-month.
